entities.description: An electronic transcription of selected Furnivall/Kempe EETS carrier matter: one 1905 series half-title and the surviving Middle English verse edition on first-pagination pp. 100–471 and reset-pagination pp. 1–356, with inherited editorial French, English paratext, apparatus, page milestones, line milestones, and italic signals.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00135
notes: TEXT/@LANG is 'enm fre'. Old French occurs in Furnivall's editorial supply and comparison layers; modern English occurs in the retained half-title, chapter arguments, summaries, and notes.
The stable name route identifies the item; direct automated inspection of the item was unavailable, while the collection page and repository XML were inspectable.

rights.basis: The current U-M collection page states that its materials are in the public domain in the United States and applies No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: Electronic text as distributed through the U-M CME collection

dateStatements.label: Composition and translation
dateStatements.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance localizes composition to London; this is distinct from the later production date and Essex dialect localization of the surviving manuscript.

entities.description: Henry Lovelich's Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the Old French Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal, narrating the origins and transmission of the Grail through Joseph of Arimathea, Josephes, converted Saracen rulers, and their descendants, and foreshadowing the later Arthurian Grail quest. The surviving text begins imperfectly.
entities.label: The History of the Holy Grail
notes: No manuscript title is asserted. The preferred title is a modern scholarly/cataloging title.
Lovelich's closing lines name him and say that he drew the story into English; nineteenth-century title-page forms 'Lonelich' are edition-layer spellings.

rights.basis: The Middle English work and its sole surviving fifteenth-century witness are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, photographs, and provider terms remain separately scoped.
rights.component: Henry Lovelich's medieval Middle English literary content

entities.description: The Old French prose Vulgate-cycle Grail history from which Lovelich translated and versified his Middle English work. The exact physical French exemplar used by Lovelich is unidentified.
entities.label: Estoire del Saint Graal
notes: The medieval English colophon and nineteenth-century editions associate Robert de Boron with translation from Latin into French. Modern source identification here remains at the anonymous Vulgate Estoire work level and does not convert that claim into confirmed modern authorship.

rights.basis: The Old French work and the fourteenth-century Royal and Additional witnesses recorded here are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, photographs, and provider terms remain separately scoped.
rights.component: Medieval Old French Estoire del Saint Graal literary content

bibliographic.citation: The History of the Holy Grail, Englisht, ab. 1450 A.D., by Herry Lonelich, skynner, from the French prose of Sires Robiers de Borron, re-edited from the unique manuscript by Frederick J. Furnivall, Parts I–IV, EETS Extra Series 20, 24, 28, 30 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1874, 1875, 1877, 1878).
bibliographic.pages: Part I pp. 1–172; Part II pp. 173–471; Part III pp. 1–208; Part IV pp. 209–361
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 20, 24, 28, 30
bibliographic.title: The History of the Holy Grail

entities.description: Furnivall's four-part EETS textual edition re-edits Lovelich's Middle English verse from CCCC MS 80, supplies the lost opening and a later lacuna from Royal MS 14 E III, and collates French manuscripts. CME00135's BODY derives from selected pages of these four parts; Dorothy Kempe's separately issued 1905 Part V study and retained half-title are modeled as a different publication entity.
entities.label: F. J. Furnivall, The History of the Holy Grail, Parts I–IV, EETS Extra Series 20, 24, 28, and 30 (1874–1878)
notes: The Middle English poem ends on Part IV p. 356; the Merlin excerpt on pp. 357–361 is not encoded.
CME00135's BODY selects Part I pp. 100–172, all Part II pp. 173–471, all Part III pp. 1–208, and Part IV pp. 209–356.

rights.basis: Furnivall's four textual-edition parts were published in 1874–1878 and are outside United States copyright term. Provider reproductions are separately scoped.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1874–1878 transcription, editorial arguments, notes, arrangement, and typography

bibliographic.citation: Dorothy Kempe, The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development, The Introduction to, and Part V of, Henry Lovelich's Verse History of the Holy Grail, EETS Extra Series 95 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1905).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 95
bibliographic.title: The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development

entities.description: Dorothy Kempe's separately issued General Introduction and Part V study. CME00135 retains only its EETS series half-title and excludes the title page, introduction, study, and other substantive matter; it is therefore not treated as co-edited continuation of Furnivall's textual Parts I–IV.
entities.label: Dorothy Kempe, The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development, EETS Extra Series 95 (1905)
notes: Only the half-title reading 'The Legend of the Holy Grail / EETS / Extra Series No XCV 1905' is retained in CME00135 FRONT.

rights.basis: Kempe's Part V study was published in 1905 and is outside United States copyright term. Provider reproductions are separately scoped.

access.entityLabel: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80
access.format: HTML landing page, MODS XML, and downloadable James catalogue record

localCopies.label: Parker MODS record for CCCC MS 80

workPortion.label: History of the Holy Grail constituent record

localCopies.label: M. R. James catalogue record for CCCC MS 80

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: Parker's summary uses c. 1450–75, while the encoded M. R. James date field and origin range are broadly fifteenth century.

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Lovelich's History of the Holy Grail and Merlin. The Grail text begins imperfectly on two displaced leaves now ff. 197–198, originally leaves 10–11 of quire B, and then continues on ff. 1r–88v. It is written in double columns in a current hand.

holding.notes: The History of the Holy Grail component is cataloged at ff. 1r–88v with the displaced surviving opening at ff. 197r–198v; Merlin continues from f. 88v to 200v.

entities.label: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80
notes: DIMEV associates the manuscript language with LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360, grid 557 181, Essex. That is a dialect localization of the surviving copy, not the London composition claim.
The surviving work locus in textual order is ff. 197r–198v, then 1r–88v; the current codex order is not the narrative order.

physicalDescription.format: Codex; double columns of 65, 69, 74, and varying numbers of lines; current hand
notes: Collation: quire A gone; quire B of 16 wants leaves 1–9, while leaves 10–11 are now ff. 197–198 and leaves 12–16 remain in situ.
Vellum guards preserve a twelfth-century manuscript with lessons from I Kings; later marginal notes include writing by John Cok.
Parker considers it likely that the manuscript remained in or around London before entering Matthew Parker's collection.
physicalDescription.support: Parker MODS gives both 'Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum'; the EETS title describes a paper manuscript

bibliographic.title: Seynt Graal, or The Sank Ryal

entities.description: Furnivall's earlier two-volume edition prints Lovelich's Middle English verse together with Old French prose and substantial prefatory and appendix matter. The later EETS publication is explicitly a re-edition without the continuous French parallel; the EETS text nevertheless states that it was re-edited from the unique manuscript, so this earlier edition is modeled as a parallel edition rather than a proven intervening transcription.

rights.basis: The two-volume edition was published in 1861–1863 and is outside United States copyright term. Provider reproductions are separately scoped.

next_steps: Collate the XML L sequence against Furnivall Parts I–IV and the Parker images, preserving the pp. 304–308 French lacuna and pp. 337–340 translator-omission insertion as non-English layers.
Determine the counting conventions behind DIMEV and Arlima before treating either number as a manuscript line count.
Do not infer missing XML verse solely from aggregate differences.
openQuestions.question: Why do the checked XML's 23,784 L elements, DIMEV's 23,932 manuscript lines, and Arlima's 23,922 verses disagree?

next_steps: Consult modern textual studies of Lovelich and the Vulgate Estoire rather than treating Royal 14 E III or Add 10292 as his exemplar merely because Furnivall used them.
Compare Lovelich's readings and omissions against multiple French Estoire witnesses.
Keep the colophon's Robert de Boron claim as medieval attribution history until modern source criticism supports a narrower responsibility statement.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Old French Estoire manuscript or textual branch did Lovelich use, and how should the medieval Robert de Boron attribution be evaluated?

next_steps: Recheck both current BL catalog records and legacy Digitised Manuscripts routes after service-recovery updates.
Ask BL Reference Services whether historical digital files or new IIIF manifests can be supplied.
Use the paid digitisation route for exact folios only if public restoration remains unavailable and costs/permissions are acceptable.
notes: Both current catalog records explicitly say digital images are unavailable; historical full-coverage notes are not current download links.
openQuestions.question: Will the British Library restore public complete facsimiles for Royal MS 14 E III and Add MS 10292, and do stable IIIF or downloadable replacements exist?

next_steps: Ask Parker Library staff to reconcile the MODS aggregate 'Paper and Vellum' statement, its component 'Vellum' statement, and Furnivall's 'paper MS' title.
Consult current conservation/codicological documentation rather than choosing a term from visual appearance alone.
Preserve the conflicting source statements in the meantime.
openQuestions.question: Is CCCC MS 80's principal support paper, vellum, or a mixed material description at different cataloging levels?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production records and source-copy identifiers.
Compare distinctive scan defects and corrected readings against available EETS copies.
Keep the current artifact-to-U-M link supported rather than claiming an undocumented scanner or exact copy.
openQuestions.question: When, by whom, and from which physical or digital copy of the EETS edition was CME00135 originally keyed?

reviewNotes: The intellectual subject is Henry Lovelich's single Middle English verse translation, not the French Estoire, the EETS five-part carrier, Dorothy Kempe's excluded 1905 study, the manuscript codex as a whole, or any digital representation.
The XML has 47 DIV1 elements: one FRONT title-page division plus 46 BODY chapter divisions numbered XI–LVI. These layers are not collapsed into a 47-chapter count.

The BODY begins at source-edition p. 100 with the first surviving Middle English line, 'For that power haven not we'. The GAP EXTENT='99 pages' represents omitted edition pp. 1–99, where Furnivall printed Old French because the English witness begins imperfectly.

A later physical loss is explicit at edition p. 304: Furnivall says the English copyist omitted a column or page. He prints Royal MS 14 E III French through p. 308, and the Middle English resumes on p. 309.

The French prose on edition pp. 337–340 is a different boundary: Furnivall supplies an explanation of the Turning Isle that he says the English translator omitted. It is not silently classified as a lost leaf of CCCC MS 80.
The XML includes 23,784 L elements. DIMEV reports 23,932 manuscript lines and Arlima reports 23,922 verses; the incompatible extents are preserved as an open question rather than normalized.

Parker's MODS record contains both aggregate 'Paper and Vellum' and component-level 'Vellum' material statements. The edition title calls the source a paper manuscript. No unsupported choice among these descriptions is made.
Private Git-ignored caches preserve complete provider objects. The Parker ZIP contains the full 414-canvas codex, not only the target folios; caching does not itself authorize redistribution.
The Parker image sequence follows current foliation, while the work's textual sequence is discontinuous: canvases 401–404 (ff. 197r–198v), then canvases 9–184 (ff. 1r–88v).

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00135. Its FRONT contains only the 1905 EETS Extra Series no. 95 half-title, while its BODY encodes the surviving Middle English verse in Frederick J. Furnivall's four-part EETS edition: first-pagination pp. 100–471 and reset-pagination pp. 1–356. The XML marks the omitted French replacement opening on edition pp. 1–99 with one GAP, and preserves Furnivall's later French supply from Royal MS 14 E III on pp. 304–308, his inserted French explanation of the Turning Isle on pp. 337–340, modern chapter arguments, marginal summaries, variants, and a terminal note from Add MS 10292. Furnivall re-edited the unique Middle English witness, Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80, whose surviving textual order is now ff. 197r–198v followed by 1r–88v. A complete 414-canvas color Parker facsimile is publicly available and cached at 1600-pixel width. Royal MS 14 E III and Add MS 10292 formerly had complete British Library digital coverage, but their images were unavailable when checked; their catalogs, physical-request form, and paid digitisation route are recorded. Furnivall's 1861–63 Roxburghe Club edition is a parallel earlier edition, not inserted as an undocumented textual intermediary between the manuscript and the EETS re-edition.

catalogingSubject.label: The History of the Holy Grail
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Henry Lovelich's 1420s Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the Old French Estoire del Saint Graal, represented by the unique, imperfect witness in Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80. The checked XML begins at the first surviving English line and ends with Lovelich's prayer for completion. The abstract work is distinguished from Furnivall's modern chapter arguments and notes, his Old French replacement and explanatory passages, the retained 1905 EETS series half-title, the separate Merlin text that follows in the manuscript, and all digital-representation layers.

assertion.notes: This work date is kept separate from the date of the surviving physical witness.
The Parker catalog also supplies the broader designation fifteenth century; the narrower range is a manuscript date, not a composition date.

The sources inspected identify Furnivall's 1861 and 1863 volumes as the earliest verified edition; 'first verified publication' avoids claiming that a still-unidentified earlier printing cannot exist. Later EETS publication dates remain in lineage only.

dateStatements.display: First verified publication in Furnivall's two-volume Roxburghe Club edition, 1861–1863

scope.description: Earliest publication of the Middle English work verified in the inspected edition and scholarly bibliography

extent.description: Cataloged at the single-work depth: one medieval verse work, with no prose-only or mixed intellectual items. Representation metrics remain separate: the XML has one FRONT half-title and 46 editorial BODY chapter divisions XI–LVI; chapters XXIV, XXV, and XXVII contain direct Old French editorial prose and chapter XXXVI contains a direct Modern English editorial summary. The XML's 49 LG containers and 23,784 L elements are retained as raw encoding metrics, not a reconciled manuscript line count: DIMEV reports 23,932 lines and another secondary count reports 23,922.

formStatements.description: A long Middle English narrative poem in rhyming couplets, predominantly four-stress lines. Furnivall's prose supplies, explanations, arguments, and notes do not make the abstract work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The narrative joins Grail and Arthurian transmission to Joseph of Arimathea, conversion, Christian kingship, miracle, prophecy, and prayer.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: This is the language of Lovelich's work. The Old French and Modern English encoded passages are edition layers.

CME00135's FRONT is only the EETS Extra Series no. 95 half-title from Dorothy Kempe's separately issued 1905 Part V study. The medieval work is represented in BODY chapters XI–LVI from Furnivall's 1874–1878 textual Parts I–IV.

The lost English opening, Furnivall's Old French replacement opening, the later manuscript lacuna, and Lovelich's omission of the Turning Isle episode are four distinct textual facts and are not collapsed into one generic gap.
The unique manuscript's correct narrative order is ff. 197r–198v followed by ff. 1r–88v; present folio order alone is misleading.
Royal MS 14 E III and Add MS 10292 are Furnivall's documented French comparison or supply witnesses. Neither is asserted as the French manuscript physically used by Lovelich.
Work-level form is verse and primary language is Middle English. Encoded Old French and Modern English prose belongs to source-edition intervention and paratext layers.

The complete 414-canvas Parker facsimile has been cached and the work's exact canvas interval is recorded; unavailable British Library facsimiles remain requestable through the routes documented in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Collate the complete XML L sequence against Furnivall Parts I–IV and the Parker images.
Separate Middle English verse from the pp. 304–308 French lacuna supply and pp. 337–340 translator-omission explanation.
Document each source's counting convention before normalizing any work-level extent.
openQuestions.question: Why do the XML's 23,784 L elements and DIMEV's 23,932 manuscript lines disagree, and how do both relate to the separate 23,922-verse count reported elsewhere?

next_steps: Consult modern textual studies of Lovelich and the Vulgate Estoire.
Compare Lovelich's readings and omissions against multiple French witnesses.
Do not promote Royal MS 14 E III, Add MS 10292, or the Robert de Boron claim into direct-source or responsibility assertions without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical Old French Estoire manuscript or textual branch did Lovelich use, and how should the poem's medieval Robert de Boron attribution be evaluated?

next_steps: Consult the current scholarship behind the Database of Middle English Romance date and place fields.
Inspect the LALME/eLALME linguistic profile and its underlying features for CamCCC80.
Retain distinct work-composition, manuscript-date, and witness-dialect assertions.
openQuestions.question: Can the 1420s London composition claim and the unique witness's Essex dialect localization be independently refined without conflating composition, copying, and linguistic localization?

assertion.notes: DIMEV links CamCCC80 to LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360 and grid 557181. Witness dialect is not silently promoted into place of composition.

scope.description: Language of the unique surviving manuscript witness

assertion.notes: The authorial responsibility applies to the Middle English poem, not to its anonymous Old French source or Furnivall's editorial prose.
Lovelich translated and adapted the Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal into Middle English rhyming couplets; his exact physical French exemplar remains unidentified.

notes: Source-edition responsibility, not medieval intellectual authorship.

scope.description: The 1874–1878 EETS source-edition representation of the poem

summaries.value: Henry Lovelich's long Middle English rhyming-couplet translation of the Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal recounts the Grail's early sacred history: Joseph of Arimathea and his followers, the conversion and adventures of Evalach and Seraphe, the Grail lineage, marvels and prophecies, and the transmission leading toward Arthurian Britain. The sole surviving manuscript begins imperfectly. CME00135's BODY follows Furnivall's four-part edition from the first surviving English line through Lovelich's closing prayer, with clearly scoped editorial French and Modern English interventions; its separate FRONT retains only a half-title from Dorothy Kempe's 1905 Part V study.

The encoded BODY has 46 numbered chapters, XI–LVI. It begins at Furnivall Part I p. 100 because the lost English opening was replaced in the edition by Old French on pp. 1–99, represented in the XML only by a GAP. The English poem continues through Part II, Part III, and Part IV p. 356. Furnivall inserts Old French on pp. 304–308 for a later physical lacuna, prints an Old French Turning Isle explanation on pp. 337–340 for material omitted by the translator, supplies one bracketed Modern English summary in chapter XXXVI, and appends a French explicit from Add MS 10292 in a TAILNOTE.

Work-level verse, Middle English language, authorship, translation, date, and place are modeled separately from manuscript production, manuscript dialect, editorial prose, source-edition carrier pages, and digital encoding. Furnivall's 1874–1878 textual Parts I–IV and Kempe's separately issued 1905 Part V study are distinct lineage entities. Royal MS 14 E III is Furnivall's documented French supply and comparison witness, not Lovelich's demonstrated exemplar. The displaced leaves in CCCC MS 80 are read in textual order as ff. 197r–198v followed by ff. 1r–88v.

assertion.notes: No title in the surviving manuscript is asserted as the preferred title.

titles.supplied_by: Modern scholarly and corpus cataloging, normalized from Furnivall's edition title

scope.description: First surviving Middle English verse line; the original opening is physically lost

titles.value: For that power haven not we

assertion.notes: The final four encoded verse lines close Lovelich's self-identifying prayer.

titles.value: And bringen this book to A Good Ende. / Now therto Iesu Crist grace me sende; / And that an Ende there-Offen myhte be, / Now, goode lord, graunt me for Charyte.

work.author: Henry Lovelich
work.dateDisplay: Translated in the 1420s; first attested c. 1450–1475; first verified publication 1861–1863
work.displayTitle: The History of the Holy Grail
work.preferredTitle: The History of the Holy Grail

publication.embeddedTitle: The Legend of the Holy Grail.

work.regionDisplay: London, England; surviving-witness dialect localized to Essex
work.summary: Henry Lovelich's long Middle English rhyming-couplet translation of the Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal recounts the Grail's early sacred history: Joseph of Arimathea and his followers, the conversion and adventures of Evalach and Seraphe, the Grail lineage, marvels and prophecies, and the transmission leading toward Arthurian Britain. The sole surviving manuscript begins imperfectly. CME00135's BODY follows Furnivall's four-part edition from the first surviving English line through Lovelich's closing prayer, with clearly scoped editorial French and Modern English interventions; its separate FRONT retains only a half-title from Dorothy Kempe's 1905 Part V study.
